summary: The narrowest workable slice was to extend `summarize` rather than invent new benchmark tasks. Existing repeated-run families already covered the trust surfaces; the missing piece was a stable scorecard and cache-aware metrics.
ergonomics: `benchmarks/run-benchmark.mjs` is centralized enough that new scorecards can be added locally without touching the runner protocol.
`benchmarks/test-series-summary.sh` is effective for end-to-end regression coverage, but its synthetic fixture block is long and easy to miscalculate when adding new derived metrics.
The existing `uncachedTotal` plumbing made cache-aware reporting straightforward once cached-input medians were added.
follow_ups: Factor scorecard definitions and printers out of `benchmarks/run-benchmark.mjs` before adding more benchmark programs; the file is getting dense around summarize-time reporting.
Consider adding schema-level validation for optional `harnessUsage` cache fields so missing cache data is surfaced explicitly instead of silently degrading to zero/`n/a`.
If this benchmark track grows, add a dedicated test helper for synthetic result generation so new repeated-run scenarios do not require hand-editing large bash fixture sections.
warnings: `summarizeGroup` uses rounded medians, so cache-rate assertions need fixture values chosen to survive rounding cleanly.
`timeToGreenMs` is based on the first passing run in order, not the median runtime across all runs; scorecard expectations need to reflect that.
`scripts/verify-all.sh` emits a lot of dirty-tree warnings from Nix/Git detection; those are noisy but not failures.
residual_risks: The new scorecard is a reporting layer over existing task families; it does not introduce new standalone task manifests, so future benchmark drift in those source families will change this scorecard indirectly.
Cache reuse metrics depend on `harnessUsage.cachedInputTokens` and `uncachedInputTokens` being populated consistently by harness adapters.
